risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization at the family level was done after obtaining consent from and completing baseline measurements on participating families. Families were stratified at each tribal community on the basis of child BMI percentile for age and gender, with two strata at each community: families with children BMIs < 85th percentile and families with children BMIs ≥ 85th percentile. Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used to ensure that there was an equal number of families in the intervention and control groups." Comment: This describes stratified and blocked randomization with random assignment within strata, which is sufficient to indicate a proper random sequence generation method. Although the exact generator is not named, the reported procedure clearly indicates random allocation rather than a predictable or non-random rule.

Quote: "Randomization at the family level was done after obtaining consent from and completing baseline measurements on participating families. Families were stratified... Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used..." Comment: The article describes stratified/blocked randomization, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation or sealed opaque envelopes) preventing recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment. Therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During Year 1, intervention families were also invited with their extended family to three mentor-led group sessions" and "During Year 1, control families received educational tool kits and incentives sent home on the same cycle as intervention families, but they did not receive any mentoring." Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded; the intervention involved obvious mentor-led home visits/group sessions versus mailed materials only. Because the study outcomes included behavior-related measures (nutrition, physical activity, BMI, questionnaires) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, this domain is at high risk of performance bias.

Summary: The article reports that anthropometrics were measured with standardized equipment ("Height was measured with a portable stadiometer... Weight was measured with a calibrated electronic scale") and that dietary recalls were "obtained by trained study personnel"; accelerometry and laboratory assays were also used for some outcomes. However, it does not state whether the outcome assessors were blinded to allocation. The study also includes a mix of objective outcomes (BMI, accelerometry, lab values) and subjective/self-reported outcomes (24-hour dietary recalls, screen time, questionnaires), so without explicit blinding information the risk of detection bias cannot be determined confidently.

Summary: The paper explicitly notes the recruitment target was set to retain 120 families from 150 enrolled, and although it states that retaining families for 2 years is difficult, it does not report any signal of differential dropout or outcome-related exclusions between intervention and control groups. The methods indicate a 2-year follow-up with repeated measures and no indication that missing data were handled in an inappropriate way; overall, the report suggests reasonable follow-up and no obvious attrition bias signal. Comment: On the information provided, attrition does not appear large or clearly imbalanced enough to bias the results, so a permissive Low-risk judgement is appropriate.
